questions.questionText: Little's area (Kiesselbach's plexus) is located on the:
questions.optionB: Anteroinferior part of nasal septum (convergence of 4 arteries — most common site of epistaxis, especially in children)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Little's area = anteroinferior septum = most common site of epistaxis. Why correct: Kiesselbach's plexus: anastomosis of 4 arteries — anterior ethmoidal, sphenopalatine, superior labial, greater palatine. Rich vascular plexus on anterior septum → most common nosebleed site (90% of epistaxis). Why A wrong: Lateral wall has turbinates and meatus but not Little's area. Why C wrong: Floor of nose — not the primary site. Why D wrong: Roof contains cribriform plate (olfactory area). Clinical relevance: Anterior epistaxis: common in children, usually from Little's area. Posterior epistaxis: from sphenopalatine artery, in elderly/hypertensives — more difficult to control, may need posterior nasal packing or arterial embolization. Exam trap: Woodruff's plexus = posterior epistaxis area (sphenopalatine artery on posterior lateral wall). Memory aid: 'Little's area = Little children's nosebleed spot = anteroinferior Septum.' Reference: Dhingra – Nose Anatomy.
questions.conceptTag: Little area Kiesselbach plexus epistaxis septum
